axes.name: boundary
axes.justification: All 6 files (4 new + 2 modified) belong cleanly to the PG-002 migration runner scope. No unrelated config changes, no scope creep into adapter implementation or Effect runtime. The plan correctly scopes to: migration runner script, PG drizzle config, tests, npm scripts, and CI job. The rollback plan demonstrates clean boundaries ā€” revert in reverse order.

axes.name: coupling
axes.justification: Dependencies are minimal and follow existing patterns. `Global.Path.data` from `@opencode-ai/core/global` is used in 26+ source files already. `#db` import for `init`/`applyMigrations` is used by `adapter.ts` (line 4) and `db.ts` (line 11). The SQLite path adds no unwanted coupling. The PG path delegates to `applyMigrations()` which is designed as a standalone function with no Effect coupling.

findings.detail: The `MIGRATIONS_PG` constant is declared but never referenced ā€” `applyMigrations()` hardcodes its own path internally (`db.pg.ts:26`). Dead constant adds confusion for future maintainers. Either remove it or pass it as an optional `migrationsFolder` param to `applyMigrations`.

axes.name: safety
axes.justification: Critical resource leak in the PG migration path. The SQLite path has proper `try/finally { sqlite.close() }` cleanup, but the PG path calls `initPg(url, ssl)` which creates a `Pool` (for non-PGlite connections) and never closes it. For real Postgres connections this leaks pool resources and holds connections open indefinitely. Additionally, no `try/finally` wrapping exists around the PG path at all ā€” an error in `applyMigrations` would leave dangling connections.

findings.detail: PG client resource leak: `initPg(url, ssl)` creates a `Pool` for remote Postgres connections (`db.pg.ts:18-23`). The pool is never closed after `applyMigrations` completes. Must add `try/finally` with `pool.end()` or equivalent cleanup. Use the same duck-typing pattern already used in `db.pg.ts:29` (`(db as any).$client`) to access the underlying pool client and call `.end()` on it if available.

Missing `try/finally` wrapping for the entire PG path. If `applyMigrations` throws partway through, the pool/connection is never cleaned up. The SQLite path has this right ā€” wrap both paths in `try/finally`.

The `init()` function exposes no way to close/end the Pool it creates. For the migration runner use case, either: (a) add an `end()` method to `PgClient`/`init()` return, or (b) document the duck-type cleanup pattern. Currently the Pool `$client.end()` is accessible only via `(db as any).$client.end()` ā€” fragile and invisible to callers.

axes.name: reversibility
axes.justification: Excellent rollback planning. All 4 new files can be independently deleted. Both modified files (`package.json`, `test.yml`) receive purely additive changes ā€” 4 new npm scripts and a new CI job appended at the end. No existing content is modified or removed. The plan explicitly lists reverse-order rollback instructions.

axes.name: surface_area
axes.justification: Minimal surface area. One new exported function (`runMigrations`), one type (`Dialect`), one interface (`MigrateOptions`). No changes to existing exports, no type signature changes. The `runMigrations` export is appropriate for programmatic use by other modules. The npm script naming (`migrate`, `migrate:pg`, `db:generate`, `db:generate:pg`) follows the existing `test:pg`, `test:ci` convention. The `db:generate` script is an alias of `db generate` ā€” existing `db` script is preserved.

findings.detail: The `db:generate` script duplicates the existing `db` script's `generate` subcommand. This is additive and doesn't break anything, but developers now have two ways to do the same thing (`bun run db generate` vs `bun run db:generate`). Consider dropping `db:generate` and only adding `db:generate:pg` since `db` still works.

axes.name: convention
axes.justification: Strong alignment with existing codebase. Uses `Global.Path.data` ā€” same pattern as `db.ts:27-35`. Uses `#db` import ā€” same as `adapter.ts:4` and `db.ts:11`. Uses `import.meta.dirname` for path resolution ā€” consistent with Bun conventions. Drizzle PG config uses `defineConfig` like existing `drizzle.config.ts`. CI job mirrors existing `e2e` job structure. The plan explicitly notes that `assert`/`node:assert` should be avoided per the codebase style (Bun test prefers `expect`).

findings.detail: The plan says `dialect: "postgresql"` ā€” confirmed correct from drizzle-kit source (`index.mjs:9` documents "postgresql" as the value). The existing PG snapshot uses `dialect: "postgres"` internally, but `defineConfig` accepts `"postgresql"`. Verified compatible.

The `detectDialect()` function only checks env vars and URL, not CLI flags. CLI flag detection is done separately in the `import.meta.main` block. This is correct separation of concerns but the plan's design table (item 1) misleadingly claims "CLI flag > env var > URL > default" as the single priority order, when in reality CLI flags override `detectDialect()` after it runs. The `runMigrations({ dialect: forceDialect })` handles this correctly ā€” `forceDialect` takes precedence because `opts.dialect ?? detectDialect()` short-circuits. Minor documentation issue.

The test uses `import { init, applyMigrations } from "#db"` ā€” this `#db` import (from `package.json` `imports` field) works in Bun for both source and test files within the `packages/opencode` package. However, no existing test file uses the `#` import syntax ā€” they all use `@/` or relative imports. This is technically fine with Bun's resolver but could confuse developers. Consider using `@/storage/db.pg` to stay consistent with test convention.

axes.name: resilience
axes.justification: Edge cases are well-documented: empty migration folders no-op, in-memory databases work, missing PG URL throws clear error, Drizzle tracks completed migrations for idempotency. The plan documents the PGlite `:memory:` path compatibility. However, the missing connection cleanup (see Safety) means connection pool exhaustion under retry/error scenarios. No timeout or backoff for PG migrations against overloaded databases.

findings.detail: No timeout mechanism for PG migrations against remote databases. A dead database or hung migration would hang the CLI indefinitely. The `test:pg` script uses `--timeout 30000` but that's only for test runner, not for the migration process itself. Consider adding an optional timeout parameter or documenting that users should wrap in their own timeout.

Concurrent migration runs against SQLite could conflict since `bun:sqlite` is synchronous but Drizzle's migrator uses async. If two processes run `migrate` simultaneously, they may race on `__drizzle_migrations`. This is true for all SQLite migration tools and is an accepted limitation, but should be documented.

conditions.description: Fix PG client resource leak in `migrate.ts` ā€” wrap PG path in try/finally and close the pool/connection after migration completes. Use duck-type check `(db as any).$client` (same pattern as `db.pg.ts:29`) and call `.end()` if available.
Remove the unused `MIGRATIONS_PG` constant from `migrate.ts` ā€” `applyMigrations()` already hardcodes the migration-pg path internally. A dead constant is maintenance debt.
Run `bun run typecheck` from `packages/opencode` after implementation to verify all imports resolve correctly, including the `#db` import in the new test file.

follow_up_issues.description: Consider extracting pool cleanup into a reusable utility or adding an explicit `disconnect()`/`close()` method to the `PgClient` type returned by `init()` in `db.pg.ts`. Current duck-type `$client.end()` approach is fragile and invisible to callers.
Add timeout/backoff guidance to the migration runner docs for PG migrations against remote databases. The current design assumes instant connection and infinite wait.

Consider whether `db:generate` script is genuinely additive or just redundant. The existing `db` script (`node ./node_modules/drizzle-kit/bin.cjs`) with the `generate` subcommand already works. `db:generate` duplicates this. `db:generate:pg` is the unique addition.
